questions.text: If there are 999 bases in an RNA that codes for a protein with 333 amino acids, and the base at position 901 is deleted, such that the length of the RNA becomes 998 bases, how many codons will be altered?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because 1 codon consists of 3 bases. Therefore, a deletion at position 901 will affect 33 codons, not 11 codons.
This option is correct because 1 codon consists of 3 bases. Therefore, a deletion at position 901 will affect 33 codons.
This option is incorrect because 1 codon consists of 3 bases. Therefore, a deletion on position 901 will affect 33 codons, not 333 codons.
This option is incorrect because 1 codon consists of 3 bases. Therefore, a deletion at position 901 will impact 33 codons, not just a single codon.

questions.explanation: In RNA, each codon is made up of three nucleotide bases, and each codon codes for a specific amino acid. So, if you delete a single base, it will result in a shift in the reading frame and potentially change the codons downstream from the deletion point. Therefore, a deletion at position 901 will affect 33 codons.

questions.text: Which of the following RNAs should be most abundant in an animal cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because tRNA comprises about 10 to 20% of the cellular RNA, while rRNA covers 80% of total RNA.
This option is incorrect because mRNA is 3 to 4% of the total RNA of a cell, while rRNA is 80%.
This option is correct because it represents the major portion of RNA in the cell, and may be up to 80% of the total RNA.

options.text: All are present in same proportion.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Because rRNA constitutes a major portion of RNA in the cell.

questions.explanation: The most abundant RNA molecule in a cell is typically ribosomal RNA (rRNA). Ribosomal RNA is a type of RNA that plays a crucial role in the ribosome, which is the cellular machinery responsible for protein synthesis.

questions.text: Spliceosomes are not found in cells of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it is eukaryotic in nature, and in eukaryotic cells, the pre-mRNA (newly synthesised mRNA) has to be modified into mature or functional mRNA before transcription, for which spliceosomes are present.

This option is incorrect because it is eukaryotic in nature, and in eukaryotic cells, the pre-mRNA (newly synthesised mRNA) has to be modified into mature or functional mRNA before transcription, for which spliceosomes are present.

This option is correct because, in prokaryotes, there is no delay between transcription and translation. As the mRNA emerges from DNA, it undergoes translation due to the absence of a definite nucleus. Hence, there is no need for spliceosomes.

This option is incorrect because it is eukaryotic in nature, and in eukaryotic cells, the pre-mRNA (newly synthesised mRNA) has to be modified into mature or functional mRNA before transcription, for which spliceosomes are present.

questions.explanation: Spliceosomes help in the removal of introns. They will not occur in prokaryotes because prokaryotes do not have introns and thus, processing does not require splicing of mRNA. Spliceosomes are complex molecular machines found only in eukaryotic cells, including those of humans and other multicellular organisms. They are responsible for a crucial process in gene expression called RNA splicing.

questions.text: The association of histone H1 with a nucleosome indicates that:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is incorrect because the association of nucleosomes and histone proteins happens after DNA replication.

options.text: The DNA is condensed into a chromatin fibre
options.explanation: This option is correct because after DNA is replicated, both DNA and histones begin to organise again, and the process of condensation continues till the cell undergoes division and the chromosomes appear.

options.text: The DNA double helix is exposed
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the H1 histone promotes condensation, meaning the DNA is tightly wound and packaged, not exposed.
This option is incorrect for the same reason as DNA replication: the DNA must be in a less condensed, more open configuration to allow the transcription machinery access to the gene sequences.

questions.explanation: Histones help in the packaging of DNA. In eukaryotes, DNA packaging is carried out with the help of positively charged basic proteins called histones. Histones are of five types – H-1, H-2A, H-2B, H-3, and H-4. H1 is attached to the linker DNA. Histone contains a large proportion of the positively charged (basic) amino acids, lysine, and arginine in its structure. DNA is negatively charged due to the phosphate groups on its backbone. The result of these opposite charges is a strong attraction and, therefore, a high binding affinity between histones and DNA.

questions.text: A molecule that can act as a genetic material must NOT fulfill which of the traits given below?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It should be able to express itself in the form of ‘Mendelian characters
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Mendel's laws state that genes are inherited from each parent, assort independently, and get segregated at an equal frequency into gametes. The inheritance of genetic material is based on the principles Mendel proposed, including autosomal dominance, autosomal recessiveness, and X-linked traits.

options.text: It should be able to generate its replica
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because genetic material must fulfil this trait. The genetic material, DNA, is capable of undergoing replication, resulting in the formation of two identical daughter cells. The enzyme DNA polymerase carries out this reaction.

options.text: It should be unstable structurally and chemically
options.explanation: This option is correct because the genetic material is stabilised using various interactions like van der Waals forces, hydrophobic interactions, and hydrogen bonds. The structural and chemical instability of the genetic material will result in impairment of cell function, mutations, and transformation to cancerous cells or diseases.

options.text: It should provide the scope for slow changes that are required for evolution
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because certain useful mutations that help the organisms survive in their changing environment get accumulated in the DNA and are passed on to the upcoming generations. A mutation in haemoglobin results in the formation of sickle-shaped red blood cells. If one copy of this defective gene is present, the individual is resistant to malaria. Two copies of this defective gene result in sickle-cell anaemia.

questions.explanation: Genetic material should be structurally and chemically stable; otherwise, its expression will change. This will lead to a loss of several metabolic functions, etc. The genetic material in any living organism consists of the fundamental information required to carry out various life processes related to development, growth, metabolism, reproduction, etc. DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid) are known to constitute the genetic material in all organisms. The genetic material is stabilised using various interactions like van der Waals forces, hydrophobic interactions, and hydrogen bonds. The structural and chemical instability of the genetic material will result in impairment of cell function, mutations, and transformation to cancerous cells or diseases.

questions.text: DNA­ dependent RNA polymerase catalyzes transcription on one strand of the DNA, which is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the template strand serves as the template for RNA synthesis (transcription).
This option is incorrect because the coding strand is a strand of DNA that is complementary to the template strand.
It is untrue because an "alpha strand" usually refers to a segment of a protein that adopts an alpha-helical conformation, contributing to the protein's overall structure.
It is untrue because an "anti-strand" usually refers to a segment of a protein that adopts an alpha-helical conformation, contributing to the protein's overall structure.

questions.explanation: The template strand serves as the template for RNA synthesis (transcription). It is also known as the antisense strand. During transcription, an enzyme called RNA polymerase reads the template strand and synthesises a complementary RNA molecule. The resulting RNA molecule is complementary to the template strand, with the exception of using uracil (U) in place of thymine (T) in RNA. The strand of DNA on which RNA polymerase binds to catalyse transcription is called the template strand. It is also known as the master or antisense strand. It has a polarity of 3' ➡ 5'.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the starter codon?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Factually incorrect, as it doesn't initiate methionine.
It is false because a starter codon must serve as the initiation point for the translation process.

This option is correct because it serves as the initiation codon and codes for the amino acid methionine in eukaryotes and, in most cases, in prokaryotes. It also signals the beginning of protein synthesis or translation in the ribosome.
Factually incorrect, it is a stop codon.

questions.explanation: Polypeptide synthesis is signaled by two initiator codons or start codons, i.e., AUG (methionine codon) and rarely by GUG (valine codon). The starter codon is "AUG." It serves as the initiation codon and codes for the amino acid methionine in eukaryotes and, in most cases, in prokaryotes. It also signals the beginning of protein synthesis or translation in the ribosome. The AUG codon is often referred to as the "start codon." The other codons mentioned, UAA, UAG, and UGA, are stop codons that signal the termination of protein synthesis.

questions.text: Which of the following is required as an inducer(s) for the expression of the Lac operon?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because in the lac operon, Lactose acts as an inducer for the synthesis of protein. Lactose binds with the repressor protein, causing an allosteric shift.

This option is incorrect because, in the presence of beta-galactosidase, lactose gets converted into glucose and galactose. Hence, lactose is an inducer in the lac operon, whereas galactose is a product.
This option is incorrect because Glucose is also an end product of the lac operon; it represses the lac operon after its production.
This option is incorrect because Galactose is the end product of the lac operon, which is formed by metabolising lactose with the help of the beta-galactosidase enzyme.

questions.explanation: In the Lac operon, lactose is an inducer. It binds with a suppressor and inactivates it. It allows RNA polymerase to access the promoter, and transcription proceeds. The lac Z, lac Y, and lac genes in the lac operon are the structural genes that code for beta-galactosidase, which converts lactose into glucose and galactose. In the lac operon, Lactose acts as an inducer for the synthesis of protein. Lactose binds with the repressor protein, causing an allosteric shift. This altered repressor cannot bind to the operator, thereby making the promoter region accessible. As the promoter region gets activated, the RNA polymerase enzyme initiates the transcription of the lac operon. Thus, lactose is required for the expression of the lac operon.

questions.text: Which one of the following does not apply to RNA?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because DNA and RNA are two different types of genetic components. Deoxyribose contains one less oxygen than ribose sugar; thus, although DNA contains thymine, RNA contains uracil.

This option is correct because the ratio of pyrimidine and purine nucleotides in DNA in any organism's cell is 1:1. As a result, this rule holds for DNA as well as some double-stranded RNA. So not apply to single-stranded RNA.

This option is incorrect because there are five different forms of nitrogenous bases in genetic material. Pyrimidine: -uracil, cytosine, and thymine, and Purine -adenine and guanine. Instead of thymine, uracil is present in RNA.
This option is incorrect because these are called complementary base pairs. In RNA, a 2' hydroxyl end is present, and in DNA, a 3' hydroxyl end is present.

questions.explanation: Chargaff’s rules apply only to double-stranded DNA molecules. These do not apply to single-stranded DNA or RNA molecules. Chargaff’s rules state that DNA helices contain equal molar ratios of A and T, G and C. This is because in a DNA molecule, complementary base pairing occurs between A and T, and C and G base pairs. This complementary base pairing is not possible in the case of a single-stranded RNA molecule. Thus, Chargaff’s rules do not apply to RNA.

questions.text: Balbiani rings are sites of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the synthesis of polysaccharides takes place in the Golgi apparatus, not in Balbiani rings.

This option is correct because the mRNAs may be briefly stored in the puff, and they may go through the process of transcription to produce proteins. Therefore, Balbiani rings are the locations of RNA and protein synthesis.

This option is incorrect because the prime site of lipid synthesis is the endoplasmic reticulum. A network of membranous tubules within the eukaryotic cell’s cytoplasm, that is continuous with the nuclear membrane, is called the endoplasmic reticulum.

This option is incorrect because the nucleotides can be further differentiated into pyrimidines and purines. In complex multicellular species, they are both mainly produced in the liver; the process is known as de novo nucleotide synthesis.

questions.explanation: In certain development stages, the polytene chromosomes bear conspicuous swellings called chromosome puffs. The larger swellings are called Balbiani rings. In the region of a puff or Balbiani ring, the DNA strands uncoil, become active, and produce a number of copies of mRNA. The mRNAs may remain temporarily stored in the puff, and they may undergo transcription to form proteins. Thus, Balbiani rings are the sites of RNA and protein synthesis.

questions.text: Identify the correct order of organization of genetic material from largest to smallest:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because A genome is a complete set of DNA that includes all of its genes, non-coding DNA, mitochondrial DNA, and chloroplast DNA. It must contain all of the information required to construct and maintain that organism. It is the largest of the options presented.

This option is incorrect because chromosomes are threadlike structures composed of protein and a single molecule of DNA that is found inside the nucleus of animal and plant cells. Because the genome is the largest, its organisation comes after it.

This option is incorrect because Chromosomes are threadlike structures composed of protein and a single molecule of DNA that is found inside the nucleus of animal and plant cells. Because the genome is the largest, its organisation comes after it.

This option is incorrect because Nucleotides are nucleic acid building blocks that act as monomeric units in the nucleic acid polymers deoxyribonucleic acid and ribonucleic acid. Nucleotides follow genes.

questions.explanation: In the genome, all the genes are contained in a single set of chromosomes. The instructions in our genome are present in the form of DNA. DNA has a complicated structure in the form of a double helix. Single strands of DNA are coiled up into structures called chromosomes. Within the chromosomes, segments of DNA are “read” together to form genes. Thus, a gene is a segment of DNA or chromosome situated at a specific locus (gene locus) that carries coded information associated with a specific function and can undergo crossing over as well as mutation. A nucleotide is the basic unit of DNA made up of a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and a nitrogenous base.

questions.text: Satellite DNA is important because it:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Codes for enzymes needed for DNA replication
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because satellite DNA does not code for any proteins or enzymes. Instead, it consists of repetitive sequences that do not serve a direct role in the coding of functional proteins.

options.text: Codes for protein needed in cell cycle
options.explanation: This option is inaccurate as satellite DNA is classified as non-coding DNA. It does not code for any proteins or enzymes involved in the cell cycle, which instead depend on various coding genes.

options.text: Does not code for proteins and is same in all members of population
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because although satellite DNA does not code for proteins, it does show high variability (polymorphism) among individuals in a population, making it unique rather than uniform across members.

options.text: Shows high polymorphism in the population and is inherited
options.explanation: This option is correct because satellite DNA exhibits significant genetic variation (polymorphism) among individuals in a population, making it useful in genetic studies and forensic identification. It is inherited from parents to offspring, contributing to individual uniqueness.

questions.explanation: Satellite DNA varies greatly among individuals. Its polymorphism is used in DNA fingerprinting, paternity tests, and forensic studies.

questions.text: Gene regulation governing the lactose operon of E.coli that involves the lac I gene product is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Negative and repressible because the repressor protein prevents transcription
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because a "repressible" system is one that is normally "on" and gets turned "off" by a corepressor, which is the opposite of the lac operon's mechanism.

options.text: Feedback inhibition occurs because an excess of β-galactosidase can switch off transcription
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Feedback inhibition typically refers to a metabolic pathway where an end product inhibits an enzyme's activity, not directly the transcription of the genes.

options.text: Positive and inducible because it can be induced by lactose
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the primary control mechanism involving the lac I repressor is negative regulation. Positive control (via the Catabolite Activator Protein, CAP) also occurs, but the question specifically asks about the lac I gene product's role, which is negative.

options.text: Negative and inducible because the repressor protein prevents transcription
options.explanation: This option is correct because the lac I gene product involved in gene control regulating the lactose operon of E.coli is negative and inducible. After all, the repressor protein prevents transcription.

questions.explanation: The control of expression of the lac operon is negative (as it is turned off normally) and inducible. An inducible operon is an operon that remains switched off normally but becomes operational in the presence of an inducer (lactose, actually allolactose, a metabolite of lactose, in the case of lac operon). The inducible operon generally functions in catabolic pathways. In the presence of an inducer, the repressor has a higher affinity for the inducer than for the operator gene. When lactose is added, a few lactose molecules are carried into the cell by the enzyme lactose permease, as a small amount of this enzyme is present in the cell even when the operon is not working. These few lactose molecules are converted into allolactose molecules, which act as an inducer and bind to the repressor (a product of the regulator gene). The repressor-­inducer complex fails to join with the operator gene, thus it is turned on.

questions.text: The diagram shows an important concept in the genetic implications of DNA. Fill in the blanks A to C:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the diagram given in the question depicts the central dogma of molecular biology, it is proposed by Francis Crick. Central dogma in molecular biology states that the genetic information flows from DNA → mRNA → proteins.

Incorrect sequence, as Translation is occurring in the diagram, not extension. The diagram given in the question depicts the central dogma of molecular biology, it is proposed by Francis Crick, not Rosalind Franklin. Central dogma in molecular biology states that the genetic information flows from DNA → mRNA → proteins.

Factually incorrect. The diagram given in the question depicts the central dogma of molecular biology, it is proposed by Francis Crick, not James Watson. Central dogma in molecular biology states that the genetic information flows from DNA → mRNA → proteins.

It is untrue. The diagram given in the question depicts the central dogma of molecular biology, it is proposed by Francis Crick, not Ervin Chargaff. Central dogma in molecular biology states that the genetic information flows from DNA → mRNA → proteins.

questions.explanation: The expression of the genetic material occurs normally through the production of proteins. This involves two consecutive steps. These are transcription and translation. The DNA codes for the production of messenger RNA (mRNA) during transcription. Messenger RNA carries coded information to ribosomes. The ribosomes read this information and use it for protein synthesis. This process is called translation. F.H.C. Crick described this unidirectional flow of information in 1958 as the ‘central dogma of molecular biology’.

questions.text: Which enzyme will be produced in a cell if there is a nonsense mutation in the lacY gene?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The production of transacetylase (encoded by the lacA gene) will be stopped due to the polar effect of the nonsense mutation in the upstream lacY gene.
Neither of these enzymes will be produced in a functional form due to the nonsense mutation in lacY and the subsequent polar effect on lacA.

This option is correct because a stop codon will be added to the sequence following the lac Z gene as a result of a nonsense mutation in the lac Y gene, as the gene Z is transcribed before the lac Y and lac A genes. Therefore, just β-galactosidase will be generated, and no other enzymes.
Lactose permease will not be produced in a functional form because its gene (lacY) is directly affected by the nonsense mutation.

questions.explanation: A nonsense mutation stops polypeptide synthesis due to the formation of a termination or non­sense codon. In the lac operon, the sequence of structural genes is Z, Y, and A, which respectively code for b-­galactosidase, lactose permease, and transacetylase. If the gene Y has a nonsense mutation, gene expression will stop at it, resulting in non­expression of both gene Y and successive gene A. Thus, only the β-galactosidase enzyme will be produced.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a property of the genetic code?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The genetic code is indeed non-overlapping, which means that each nucleotide is part of only one codon, and codons are read sequentially in sets of three.

The genetic code is unambiguous, meaning that each specific codon (a sequence of three nucleotides) always codes for the same single amino acid. It does not specify multiple amino acids. Therefore, being "ambiguous" is not a property of the genetic code.
The genetic code is degenerate (or redundant), meaning that multiple different codons can code for the same single amino acid. This property reduces the impact of some mutations.

The genetic code is largely universal, meaning that the same codons code for the same amino acids in almost all organisms, from bacteria to humans (with minor exceptions). This universality allows for genetic engineering applications like producing human insulin in bacteria.

questions.explanation: The genetic code is non-­ambiguous. A non­ ambiguous code means that there is no ambiguity about a particular code. One codon specifies only one amino acid and not any other. There are 64 codons. Out of 64, 3 are stop codons or nonsense codons, i.e., these do not code for any amino acid, and the rest of the 61 codes for one of the 20 amino acids. Neither of them codes for more than one amino acid, except for GUG, which normally codes for valine, but in certain conditions, it also codes for N-formyl methionine as the initiation codon.

questions.text: Removal of introns and joining of exons in a defined order during transcription is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because DNA looping is a process in which A DNA loop is formed when a protein or a complex of proteins simultaneously binds to two different sites on DNA.
This option is incorrect because Induction is an increase in gene expression due to the presence of an inducer.
It is false as it doesn't have a relation to the transcription process.

This option is correct because Splicing refers to the process by which the non-coding regions of genes (introns) are removed, and the coding regions (exons) are joined to form mature mRNA. The joining is performed in a defined order.

questions.explanation: Introns, which mainly occur in eukaryotes, are transcribed into messenger RNA (mRNA) but are then removed from the transcript before translation. In some cases, the removal of introns is an autocatalytic process (self-splicing) in which the RNA itself acts as an enzyme.

questions.text: If one strand of DNA has the nitrogenous base sequence as ATCTG, what would be the complementary RNA strand sequence?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it doesn't apply the pairing rule correctly.

This option is correct because pairing in strands follows the pairing principle. That is A with U and C with G. The given sequence on the DNA strand is as follows: ATCTG. Thus, the complementary one on RNA will be UAGAC.
This option is incorrect because it doesn't apply the pairing rule correctly. The complementary one on RNA will be UAGAC.
This option is incorrect because it doesn't apply the pairing rule correctly. The complementary one on RNA will be UAGAC.

questions.explanation: In RNA, thymine is substituted with uracil; thus, the RNA strand complementary to the DNA strand ATCTG will be UAGAC.

questions.text: Ribosomal RNA is actively synthesized in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Factually incorrect, because lysosomes are known as suicidal sacs, not a RNA factory.
This option is correct because the ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is synthesized and stored in the nucleolus.
It is untrue because the ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is synthesised and stored in the nucleolus, and not in the nucleoplasm.
This option is incorrect because Ribosomes are the protein factories, not RNA factories.

questions.explanation: The nucleolus is the centre for the synthesis of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) that forms ribosomal subunits. Ribosomal proteins migrate to the nucleolus from their assembly sites in the cytoplasm and are packaged into ribonucleoproteins. These return to the cytoplasm where they become mature ribosome particles.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not a part of a transcription unit in DNA?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because an inducer is a third type of regulatory molecule, which is a small molecule that either activates or represses transcription by interacting with a repressor or an activator. Its major role is in gene expression.
It is a part of transcriptional DNA. The termination region is a sequence of DNA bases trigger two events.
This option is incorrect because the start region of a gene is called the Promoter region. The promoter region of a gene is a short sequence of DNA bases located just in the 3' direction.
The part of DNA that is actually transcribed into RNA (mRNA, tRNA, or rRNA). It codes for a specific protein or RNA molecule.

questions.explanation: A transcription unit is a part of DNA that can transcribe a complete RNA. It consists of a promoter region (where RNA polymerase binds to start transcription), the structural gene (coding region), and the terminator region (that signals release of RNA polymerase and newly formed RNA strand).

questions.text: Removal of RNA polymerase III from the nucleoplasm will affect the synthesis of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because, in eukaryotic cells, RNA polymerase III performs the function of transcription of DNA to synthesise ribosomal 5S rRNA, tRNA, and other small RNAs. This enzyme complex has a more limited role than the Poly III in prokaryotes. Thus, the removal of RNA Poly III will affect the synthesis of tRNA.
This option is incorrect because RNA polymerase II synthesises mRNA and hnRNA inside the nucleus; thus, the removal of RNA polymerase III doesn't affect the synthesis of hnRNA.
This option is incorrect because RNA polymerase II synthesises mRNA and hnRNA inside the nucleus; thus, the removal of RNA polymerase III doesn't affect the synthesis of hnRNA.
This option is incorrect because RNA polymerase I synthesises rRNA inside the nucleus; thus, the removal of RNA polymerase III doesn't affect the synthesis of rRNA.

questions.explanation: In eukaryotes, RNA polymerase enzymes (Type I, II, III) catalyse the synthesis of RNA using as a template either an existing DNA strand or an RNA strand. Type I is responsible for the synthesis of rRNA, type II for mRNA, and type III for tRNA synthesis.

questions.text: Which structures give a 'beads on a string' appearance when chromosomes are viewed under an electron microscope?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Genes are the smallest unit of chromosomes. Genes are fundamental units of heredity, segments of DNA on chromosomes that carry instructions for building proteins and functional RNA.
Nucleotides are building blocks of DNA, where A, T, G, and C are found with phosphate and sugar molecules.

This option is correct because a nucleosome is the most fundamental, repeating structural element of chromatin. The chromatin structure resembles beads on a thread when expanded and seen under a microscope.
Factually incorrect. There are four bases: Adenine (A), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C), and Thymine (T). These bases are found in pairs on the DNA where A pairs with T and C pairs with G.

questions.explanation: Nucleosomes appear as ‘beads ­on a ­string’ in the chromosomes when viewed under an electron microscope. The beads in ‘beads ­on a ­string’ arrangement are complexes of histones and DNA. The beads, plus the connecting DNA that leads to the next bead, form the nucleosome. A nucleosome is the fundamental unit of organisation on which the higher-order packaging of chromatin is built. The bead of each nucleosome contains eight histone molecules, in which two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4 are found.

questions.text: The unequivocal proof of DNA as the genetic material came from the studies on a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While bacteria (E. coli) were used as the host cells in the Hershey and Chase experiment, the definitive proof came from studying the virus that infected them, not the bacterium itself. Earlier experiments by Griffith and Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty using bacteria had suggested that DNA was the genetic material, but these were not widely accepted as "unequivocal proof" until the Hershey and Chase experiment.
Fungi were not the key organisms used in the experiments that provided the definitive proof of DNA as the genetic material.

Viroids are small, circular RNA molecules that infect plants and lack a protein coat. They were not part of the experiments (Hershey and Chase) that provided the unequivocal proof for DNA as the genetic material in general.

This option is correct because the unequivocal proof that DNA is the genetic material came from the experiments performed by Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase. They worked with bacteriophages (virus that infects bacteria).

questions.explanation: Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty first established that DNA was the genetic material by working on the same bacteria as Griffith. However, they could not convince all the biologists. The unequivocal proof that DNA is the genetic material came from the experiments performed by Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase. They worked with bacteriophages (virus that infects bacteria). The unequivocal proof that DNA is the genetic material came from the experiments of Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase (1952). They worked with viruses that infect bacteria called bacteriophages.

questions.text: Which one of the following does not follow the central dogma of molecular biology?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Peas are plants with cells that follow the central dogma of molecular biology.
This option is incorrect because Mucor is a type of fungus with cells that also follow the central dogma of molecular biology.
This option is incorrect because Chlamydomonas is a single-celled green alga, and its cells follow the central dogma of molecular biology.

This option is correct because HIV is a retrovirus that does not follow the Central Dogma of molecular biology. Retroviruses have RNA as their genetic material. This conversion of RNA → DNA does not follow the central dogma of molecular biology.

questions.explanation: The one­way flow of information from DNA to mRNA and then to protein is known as the central dogma of molecular biology by F.H.C. Crick (1958). But later on, two American workers, H. Temin and D. Baltimore, reported that DNA is also formed from RNA in retroviruses such as HIV. This is called reverse transcription. This reverse transcription occurs under the influence of the reverse transcriptase enzyme. So, HIV does not follow the central dogma.

questions.text: Select the two correct statements out of the four (i-iv) statements given below about the lac operon.(i) Glucose or galactose may bind with the repressor and inactivate it.(ii) In the absence of lactose, the repressor binds with the operator region.(iii) The z-­gene codes for permease.(iv) This was elucidated by Francois Jacob and Jacques Monod.The correct statements are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Instead of (iii), (iv) is correct. The lac operon is an operon responsible for the transport and metabolism of lactose in Escherichia coli and other bacteria.
Factually incorrect. The lac operon is an operon responsible for the transport and metabolism of lactose in Escherichia coli and other bacteria.

The lac operon is an operon responsible for the transport and metabolism of lactose in Escherichia coli and other bacteria. The mechanisms of regulation of the expression of genes responsible for coding the involved enzymes were first explained by Francois Jacob and Jacques Monod. The beta-galactoside permease gene is responsible for the transport of lactose into the cell and is encoded by the lac Y-gene, not by the z-gene. Statement 3 is incorrect. In the absence of lactose sugar, the repressor protein binds to the operator and thereby does not allow the transcription of the three lac genes. Statement 2 is correct. In the presence of lactose, allolactose, an isomer of lactose, binds to the repressor and causes an allosteric shift. The altered repressor cannot bind to the operator, allowing RNA polymerase to transcribe the lac genes. Glucose/ galactose are the preferred sugars, and their presence inhibits the expression of the lac operon by catabolite repression. This sugar does not bind to a repressor. Statement 1 is incorrect. The beta-galactosidase enzyme, encoded by the lacZ gene, converts lactose into allolactose. So, the correct answer is '2 and 4'.
They are wrong options. The lac operon is an operon responsible for the transport and metabolism of lactose in Escherichia coli and other bacteria.

questions.explanation: The two French scientists, Jacob and Monod, proposed the lac operon of E.coli. The lac operon (an inducible operon) contains a promoter, an operator, a regulator gene, and three structural genes z, y, and a, coding for the enzyme b­-galactosidase, b­-galactoside permease, and b-­galactoside, respectively.

questions.text: The 3′-5′ phosphodiester linkages in a polynucleotide chain join:
questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Whose experiments cracked the DNA and discovered unequivocally that the genetic code is a “triplet”?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase conducted landmark 1952 experiments using bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria) to prove that DNA, not protein, is the genetic material, showing that only the DNA entered the host cell during infection, a finding crucial for understanding inheritance.

Thomas Hunt Morgan and Alfred Sturtevant were pioneering geneticists who, working with fruit flies (Drosophila), established the concept of gene linkage and created the first genetic map, showing genes are arranged linearly on chromosomes, with recombination frequency indicating their relative distances.

George Beadle and Edward Tatum were pioneering American geneticists who, through experiments with *Neurospora mold in the 1940s, established the "One Gene-One Enzyme Hypothesis," demonstrating that genes control specific biochemical reactions by directing the synthesis of enzymes.

Heinrich Matthaei were pioneering scientists who, in 1961, performed a landmark experiment that cracked the first piece of the genetic code, showing that the RNA sequence UUU codes for the amino acid phenylalanine, launching the race to decipher the full triplet code and revealing how DNA's instructions translate into proteins.

questions.explanation: The semi-conservative replication of DNA was first experimentally demonstrated by Matthew Meselson and Franklin Stahl in 1958. They used the bacterium Escherichia coli (E. coli) for their classic experiment, in which they used heavy nitrogen isotope (15N) and light nitrogen isotope (14N) to label and track the DNA strands across generations. The results of their density gradient centrifugation analysis confirmed the prediction of the Watson-Crick model that DNA replication is semi-conservative.

questions.text: In the DNA molecule:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The proportion of adenine in relation to thymine varies with the organism
options.explanation: It is incorrect because the mentioned statement has no basis.

options.text: There are two strands which run antiparallel­; one in the 5' ➡ 3' direction and the other in 3' ➡ 5' direction
options.explanation: The correct option is B. Two strands run antiparallel, one in a 5' → 3' direction and the other in a 3' → 5' direction. The double-stranded DNA molecule has 2 strands. One runs in the 5'→ 3' direction and the other strand runs in the 3'→5' direction. The pyrimidine A always bonds with the purine T in the complementary strand. The pyrimidine G always binds with the purine C. Therefore, the total number of purines is always equal to the total number of pyrimidines in the DNA molecule. Thus, the proportion of adenine to thymine is always the same in all organisms.

options.text: The total amount of purine nucleotides and pyrimidine nucleotides is not always equal
options.explanation: It is false as the total amount of purine nucleotides and pyrimidine nucleotides is always equal.

options.text: There are two strands which run parallel in the 5' ➡ 3' direction
options.explanation: Factually incorrect, because they both run anti-parallel. One in the 5' ➡ 3' direction and the other in the 3' ➡ 5' direction.

questions.explanation: A DNA molecule has two unbranched complementary strands, which are spirally coiled. The two chains are antiparallel, i.e., they run parallel but in opposite directions. One chain has the polarity 5' ➡ 3', whereas the other has 3' ➡ 5'. Both are held together by hydrogen bonds between their bases, i.e., A = T and G = C, and the amount of adenine is equal to thymine, and guanine equals cytosine. The base ratio A + T / G + C may vary from one species to another, but is constant for a given species. The purine and pyrimidines are always in equal amounts (A + G = T + C), but A + T is not necessarily equal to G+C.

questions.text: During transcription, holoenzyme RNA polymerase binds to a DNA sequence, and the DNA assumes a saddle-like structure at that point. What is that sequence called?

options.explanation: AAAT is not a consensus sequence for the promoter region of eukaryotic genes.

The DNA sequence that holoenzyme RNA polymerase binds to during transcription, and the DNA assumes a saddle-like structure at that point, is called the TATA box. The TATA box is a consensus sequence, meaning that it is a sequence of nucleotides that is found in the promoter region of many eukaryotic genes. It is located about 10 base pairs upstream of the transcription start site. The TATA box is important for transcription because it helps to position the RNA polymerase in the correct place to start transcription. questions.text: In a DNA, the percentage of thymine is 20%. What will be the percentage of guanine?

In a DNA, the percentage of guanine is equal to the percentage of cytosine, and the percentage of adenine is equal to the percentage of thymine. This is because the two strands of DNA are complementary, meaning that A always pairs with T, and G always pairs with C. If the percentage of thymine is 20%, then the percentage of adenine must also be 20%. This means that the percentage of guanine + cytosine must be 60%. Since the percentage of cytosine is equal to the percentage of guanine, the percentage of guanine must be 30%.

questions.text: The Pneumococcus experiment proves that:

Transformation was first discovered by Griffith (1928) in Pneumococcus (Streptococcus pneumoniae), which causes pneumonia. Griffith injected a group of mice with nonencapsulated, rough (R), pneumococci; a second group with heat­killed encapsulated pneumococci cells, and a third group a mixture consisting of a few living nonencapsulated, rough pneumococci derived from a type S culture, and heat­killed encapsulated cells (S type). Griffith observed that the mice in the first two groups were not infected, and the mice in the third group died within a few days. The mice of the third group should have survived as the organisms that could kill them had been killed, and the cells of the R type were incapable of causing disease. However, the mice died, and living virulent encapsulated cells of the type S were recovered from their dead bodies. It was observed by Griffith that killed encapsulated pneumococci had liberated some substance which favoured non­capsulated cells (R type) to produce a capsular substance. This substance, in later experiments, was proved to be DNA. These experiments showed that DNA is the genetic material.

questions.text: Which of the following steps of translation does not consume a high energy phosphate bond?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Protein synthesis or translation consists of ribosomes, amino acids, mRNA, tRNA, and aminoacyl tRNA synthetases. The ribosomes have two binding sites, namely the aminoacyl site or A-­site and the peptide site or P­-site. The starting amino acid methionine lies at the P­-site of the ribosome. The next incoming tRNA is called aminoacyl tRNA, and is bound to the A­-site. A peptide bond is formed between the COOH group of the tRNA at the P-­site and the NH2 group of the aminoacyl tRNA. This is facilitated by the enzyme peptidyl transferase and does not require high-energy phosphate bonds.

Aminoacyl binding to tRNA requires energy. This process is called aminoacylation, and it is catalysed by aminoacyl tRNA synthetases. Aminoacyl tRNA synthetases are a group of enzymes that attach amino acids to their corresponding tRNAs.
Translocation requires energy. Translocation is the movement of the ribosome along the mRNA molecule during protein synthesis.
Amino acid activation requires energy. Amino acid activation is an energy-intensive process because it requires the formation of a high-energy bond between the amino acid and the tRNA.

questions.explanation: ATP/GTP is consumed during amino acid activation, tRNA binding, and translocation. Peptide bond formation by peptidyl transferase does not require energy.

The cytoplasm is the watery part of the cell that is located outside of the nucleus. It contains a variety of organelles, including the ribosomes.

The nucleolus is a dense region within the nucleus that is responsible for ribosome biogenesis. The mRNA is transcribed from DNA in the nucleus by an enzyme called RNA polymerase. The mRNA is then transported out of the nucleus to the cytoplasm, where it is translated into proteins by ribosomes.

options.explanation: Depressor is a synonym for repressor. It doesn't trigger transcription in the lac operon.
A controlling element is a general term for any element that can regulate gene expression.
A regulator is a general term for any protein that regulates gene expression.

An environmental agent that triggers transcription from an operon is an inducer. An inducer is a molecule that binds to a repressor protein and changes its shape. This prevents the repressor protein from binding to the operator region of the operon, which allows RNA polymerase to bind to the promoter region and initiate transcription. Inducers are often the substrates of the enzymes that are encoded by the operon. This means that the operon is only turned on when the inducer is present, which ensures that the cell does not produce more of the enzyme than it needs. For example, the lac operon in E. coli is turned on by the inducer lactose. The lac operon encodes the enzymes that are needed to metabolise lactose. When lactose is present in the environment, it binds to the repressor protein and changes its shape, which allows RNA polymerase to bind to the promoter region and initiate transcription of the operon.

questions.text: Proteins that help to open up the DNA double helix in front of the replication fork are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: DNA Ligase is involved in joining DNA fragments by forming phosphodiester bonds, particularly during DNA replication and repair, but it does not open up the DNA double helix. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

DNA Topoisomerase I helps to relieve torsional strain in the DNA molecule but does not directly open up the DNA double helix. It acts by making transient cuts and allowing the DNA to rotate, reducing supercoiling. Hence, this option is incorrect.

DNA Gyrase is a type of topoisomerase that introduces negative supercoils into DNA, particularly in prokaryotes, to alleviate torsional stress. It does not directly open up the double helix, making this option incorrect.

Helicase is the enzyme responsible for unwinding the DNA double helix ahead of the replication fork by breaking hydrogen bonds between the nucleotide bases, facilitating DNA replication. This is the correct option.

questions.explanation: Helicase unwinds the DNA double helix by breaking hydrogen bonds, providing single-stranded templates.

questions.text: In protein synthesis, the polymerisation of amino acids involves three steps. Which one of the following is not involved in the polymerization of protein?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is the last step where the translation process ends. Termination in protein synthesis (translation) occurs when the ribosome encounters one of three stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA) on the mRNA, triggering protein release factors (RFs) to bind, which causes the ribosome to hydrolyse the bond between the completed polypeptide and its tRNA, releasing the new protein and disassembling the translation machinery for reuse.

The process of translation actually begins with the formation of the initiation complex. Initiation in protein synthesis is the crucial first step where the small ribosomal subunit, an initiator tRNA (carrying methionine), and the messenger RNA (mRNA) assemble to form a complex that finds the start codon (AUG) on the mRNA.
In this phase, ribosomal units move along mRNA and amino acids are brought by tRNA, which are joined together to form polypeptide chain.

Transcription is the crucial first step of protein synthesis, where a gene's DNA sequence is copied into a messenger RNA (mRNA) molecule, carried out by RNA polymerase in the nucleus, essentially creating a mobile, single-stranded blueprint (mRNA) from the permanent DNA master copy to direct protein building (translation) later in the cytoplasm at ribosomes.

questions.explanation: Transcription is the mechanism of copying the message of DNA into RNA with the help of the enzyme RNA polymerase. It is meant for taking the coded information from DNA to the site where it is required for protein synthesis. Translation or protein synthesis is a complicated process involving several steps: the activation of amino acids, the transfer of amino acids to tRNA, initiation of polypeptide synthesis, elongation of the polypeptide chain, and the termination of the polypeptide chain.

questions.text: Because most of the amino acids are represented by more than one codon, the genetic code is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is untrue because representation by more than one codon does not cause overlapping. Overlapping occurs when a single nucleotide is part of multiple codons (a very rare scenario in most biological systems), while degeneracy simply means the genetic code has redundancy built in.

Factually incorrect, because representation by more than one codon does not cause wobbling. Wobble pairing refers to the flexibility in base pairing between the 3' end of a codon and the 5' end of an anticodon, which allows a single anticodon to recognise more than one synonymous codon.

This option is correct because the Degeneracy of genetic codons means a specific amino acid can be coded by more than one triplet codon. For example, glutamic acid can be coded by GAA and GAG codons. Leucine is coded by UUA, UUG, CUU, CUC, CUA, and CUG codons. Serine is coded by UCA, UCG, UCC, UCU, AGU, and AGC.

The term "generation" would be factually incorrect in this context, as it doesn't describe the biological phenomenon of multiple codons coding for a single amino acid. The correct and accepted scientific term is indeed degeneration or degeneracy.

questions.explanation: Certain amino acids are identified by more than one codon. Some amino acids are encoded by up to four codons, and amino acids, arginine and lysine are encoded by six codons. This is called code degeneracy.

This option is incorrect because Topoisomerases (also known as DNA topoisomerases) are enzymes involved in the over- or underwinding of DNA. Topoisomerases bind to DNA and cleave the phosphate backbone of one or both DNA strands to prevent and rectify various types of topological defects produced by the double helix.

This option is correct because DNA is a double-stranded molecule. Therefore, for replication to happen, the two strands need to be separated. This is done by the enzyme helicase, which opens up the double helix for the replication process to begin.

This option is incorrect because DNA gyrase is an ATP-dependent bacterial enzyme that catalyses the negative supercoiling of double-stranded closed-circular DNA. Gyrase is a member of the topoisomerase family of enzymes, which are involved in the regulation of DNA topological transitions.

Triple-stranded and Four-stranded: These are less common forms, but the principle of complementary base pairing would still apply in any paired regions, leading to equal amounts of specific base pairs within those structures. questions.text: Nucleotide arrangement in DNA can be seen by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because X-ray crystallography can be used to see the nucleotide arrangement in DNA. It is an experimental science that can determine the atomic and molecular structure of a crystal by a beam of incident X-rays. The beam passes through the crystalline structure and gets diffracted into many specific directions. By measuring the angles and intensities of the diffracted light, one can determine the three-dimensional picture of the density of electrons inside the crystal. That's how the nucleotides are visualised.
An electron microscope (EM) cannot typically image the specific arrangement of individual nucleotides in a DNA sequence with enough resolution to "read" the genetic code.
An ultracentrifuge separates biological molecules based on differences in their size, shape, and density using high centrifugal forces.

Light microscopes use visible light to resolve images of cells, tissues, and some larger sub-cellular structures, with a maximum magnification typically around 1000x to 1500x. This is sufficient for observing things like the general shape of bacteria or the structure of a cell's nucleus.

questions.explanation: Nucleotide arrangement in DNA can be seen by X­ray crystallography. Watson and Crick, in 1953, proposed the double-helical model for DNA. They were awarded the Nobel Prize in 1962. This model was developed by them based on several previous observations, including the X­ ray reflection studies of Franklin and Gosling in 1953.

questions.text: The experimental material in the study of DNA replication has been:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the semi-conservative replication of DNA was proved by Meselson and Stahl by conducting experiments using Escherichia coli. They performed the DNA replication experiment by incorporating a heavy isotope of nitrogen (15N) into the DNA of Escherichia coli.
Neurospora crassa (a red bread mold) was famously used by Beadle and Tatum in their experiments that led to the "one gene–one enzyme" hypothesis, but not the key experiments on DNA replication.

Pneumococcus (Streptococcus pneumoniae) was the organism used in Griffith's transforming principle experiment, which was a stepping stone in identifying DNA as the hereditary material, but the key DNA replication studies used E. coli.

Drosophila melanogaster (fruit fly) is a model organism for genetic and developmental studies in eukaryotes, but the initial, fundamental studies of DNA replication were conducted in the simpler prokaryotic system of E. coli.

questions.explanation: The experimental material in the study of DNA replication has been Escherichia coli. E. coli fully labelled with 1 N is allowed to grow in a 14N medium. The two strands of the DNA molecule of the first-generation bacteria have different densities and do not resemble their parent DNA. Meselson and Stahl, in 1958, by using 14 N and 15 N confirmed that the replication of DNA in E. coli is semi­conservative in nature.

Euchromatin allows for easier access to the genetic information contained in the DNA, making it available for gene expression and various cellular processes. In contrast, heterochromatin is the condensed part of a chromosome, and it is typically in a closed configuration, which is associated with gene silencing and a lack of active transcription.

However, it is the rRNA that plays a central role in the actual process of peptide bond formation and protein synthesis.

questions.text: In what direction can a DNA polymerase work when catalyzing the addition of nucleotide monomers?

options.text: From the 5’ toward the 3’ end of the new strand
options.explanation: DNA replication takes place at a Y-shaped structure called a replication fork. A self-correcting DNA polymerase enzyme catalyses nucleotide polymerisation in a 5′-to-3′ direction, copying a DNA template strand with remarkable fidelity. Thus, the correct option is A.

options.text: From the replication centers in two directions called replication forks
options.explanation: While replication does proceed bidirectionally from an origin, creating two replication forks, the direction in which the polymerase itself works on the new strand is exclusively 5' to 3'. The leading strand is synthesised continuously in the direction of the fork, and the lagging strand is synthesised in fragments (Okazaki fragments) in the opposite direction relative to the fork movement, but still in the 5' to 3' direction for each fragment.

options.text: From the 3’ to the 5’ end of the strand being assembled
options.explanation: DNA polymerase cannot synthesise a new strand in the 3' to 5' direction because it requires the energy from the incoming nucleotide's triphosphate group to attach to the 3'-OH of the previous nucleotide. A 3' to 5' mechanism would not allow for the necessary energy release and proofreading capability.

options.text: In both directions if DNA ligase is present
options.explanation: DNA ligase is an enzyme that joins the Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand by sealing the nicks (gaps) in the sugar-phosphate backbone, but it does not change the fundamental directionality of DNA polymerase activity. The polymerase still only works 5' to 3'.

questions.explanation: DNA polymerase adds nucleotides to the 3′-OH end, meaning the new strand grows in the 5′ → 3′ direction.

This process results in a continuous and complete lagging strand.

options.text: One or more places where there is a specific sequence of nucleotides
options.explanation: This option is correct because the process of DNA replication begins at one or more places where there is a specific sequence of nucleotides. These specific sequences where DNA replication initiates are known as origins of replication. The DNA molecule is unwound, and the replication machinery assembles at these specific sites, allowing for the controlled and accurate duplication of the DNA.

questions.explanation: The process of DNA replication begins at one or more places where there is a specific sequence of nucleotides. These specific sequences where DNA replication initiates are known as the origins of replication. The DNA molecule is unwound, and the replication machinery assembles at these specific sites, allowing for the controlled and accurate duplication of the DNA. The presence of origins of replication ensures that DNA replication occurs at the right place and at the right time in the cell cycle. The exact locations of origins of replication vary between organisms, and the specific DNA sequence recognised at these origins can differ, but they are always specific and serve as starting points for DNA replication.

questions.text: As a result of replication, parental DNA would become completely dispersed and that strand of all the daughter molecules would be a mixture of old and new DNA. This is known as the _:

questions.text: Meselson and Stahl transferred a few bacteria grown in N15 medium to N14 medium for replicating their DNA. What would be the result after rounds of replication?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because after transferring bacteria grown in N-15 medium to N-14 medium and allowing for another round of replication, the result would be 50% hybrid duplex (one old heavy strand and one new light strand) and 50% light duplex (both strands containing the lighter isotope, N-14). The heavy duplex (both strands containing N-15) would not be present.
This percentage is incorrect for any generation in the N-14 medium. Heavy duplexes (N-15/N-15) are only present before any replication in the N-14 medium.
This is the result before any replication in the N-14 medium (parental generation).
This is the result after exactly one round of replication in the N-14 medium.

questions.explanation: When Meselson and Stahl conducted their famous experiment, they used bacteria grown in a heavy isotope of nitrogen (N-15) medium, and then they transferred these bacteria to a medium containing the lighter isotope of nitrogen (N-14) for replication. They were investigating the mode of DNA replication, specifically whether it followed a conservative or semi-conservative mechanism. The outcome of the experiment showed that after one round of replication, the DNA consisted of hybrid duplexes, with one strand being the original heavy strand (N-15) and one strand being the newly synthesised light strand (N-14). This result supported the semi-conservative model of DNA replication. After another round of replication, you would expect to see a mixture of DNA molecules. The result would be 50% hybrid duplex (one old heavy strand and one new light strand) and 50% light duplex (both strands containing the lighter isotope, N-14). The heavy duplex (both strands containing the heavier isotope, N-15) would not be present because the bacteria were no longer being cultured in the N-15 medium.

questions.text: If the sequence in DNA is CCCTAGAG, then what would be the sequence in messenger RNA after transcription?

options.explanation: C pairs with G, A pairs with T in DNA, while A pairs with U in RNA. The question asks for the corresponding mRNA sequence after transcription of the DNA sequence. The C bases will correspond to G bases on the mRNA, T will correspond to A, G will correspond to C, whereas A will correspond to U and not T because the transcript is made up of RNA and not DNA, hence U takes the place of T.
Only Option A has the correct order of base pairs. This is not the correct sequence. C pairs with G, A pairs with T in DNA, while A pairs with U in RNA.
